shortDescription.text: Arbitrary file read via user-controlled path passed directly to open()

fullDescription.text: The 'file' query parameter from the HTTP request is passed directly to Python's open() built-in without any path validation, sanitization, or restriction. An attacker can read any file accessible to the application process, including /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow (if permissions allow), application source code, environment files, and secrets.

shortDescription.text: No path traversal prevention or directory restriction on file read

fullDescription.text: The endpoint performs no checks against path traversal sequences (e.g., ../, ..\), no allowlist of permitted directories, no canonicalization of the path, and no chroot or sandboxing. This allows reading files anywhere on the filesystem using relative or absolute paths.

shortDescription.text: Exception message leaks filesystem information to the user

fullDescription.text: When open() raises an exception (e.g., FileNotFoundError, PermissionError), the full exception message is returned in the HTTP response. This reveals absolute file paths, permission details, and filesystem structure to the attacker, facilitating further exploitation.

message.text: User-supplied 'file' query parameter is passed directly to open() without any validation. An attacker can read arbitrary files on the server (e.g., /etc/passwd, application secrets, .env files) by sending GET /lfi?file=/etc/passwd.

snippet.text:
with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
                content = f.read()

message.text: No path validation exists between receiving the user input (line 406) and using it in open() (line 411). There is no allowlist, no directory restriction, no normalization via os.path.realpath(), and no check for path traversal sequences. Both absolute paths (/etc/passwd) and relative traversal (../../etc/passwd) succeed.

The except block formats the exception into the HTTP response via f"Error reading file: {e}". For FileNotFoundError this reveals the full absolute path; for PermissionError it reveals filesystem permissions. This information disclosure aids attacker reconnaissance.

snippet.text:
except Exception as e:
            content = f"Error reading file: {e}"
